entries.title: Roads Before Roads Became Obvious
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText:
“All roads lead to Rome,” insisted one medieval European proverb. (In other parts of the world, roads led elsewhere.) While Roman roads may be the most famous for their longevity and scale, they were far from the first road network. Explore the history of roads and the related terms below. Then, consider some of the most famous roads in the world both today and in ancient times.

macadam | pavement | asphalt | corduroy road | gallery road
holloway | ridgeway | milestones | trackway
entries.studentExplanation:
Roads did not begin with Rome, but Roman roads became famous because they made movement durable, repeatable, and politically useful across huge distances. This entry asks students to see roads as more than pavement: a road can be engineered from stone, formed from logs, worn into a hillside, marked by milestones, or remembered as a legendary route. Roads are technologies of movement, but also technologies of power, trade, memory, and control.

The added road history shows that roads are many technologies: Roman stone routes, Persian courier roads, Inca mountain networks, corduroy logs, gallery roads, holloways, ridgeways, macadam, asphalt, milestones, and trackways all solve different terrain, speed, and power problems. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand that roads are many different solutions to the same problem: how to make movement possible again and again. At the section level, it opens Where We’re Going, We’ll Still Need Them by showing why future movement still depends on old physical routes. At the year-theme level, it connects to routes, journeys, and progress because getting “there” usually requires an infrastructure someone built, maintained, inherited, or wore into the landscape.
entries.takeaway: They organize power, trade, memory, and movement across space
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether infrastructure is just practical support or also a form of historical power, memory, and control.
entries.counterargument: Roman roads deserve attention, but road history is broader than one empire and includes routes that emerged through local use, terrain, and need.

entries.sectionTitle: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them

entries.title: Roads That Think, Charge, Heal, and Drain
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText:
Modern engineering is moving towards roads that do more than just sit on the ground—they participate actively in transport. As you explore the innovations below, consider: what problems are they trying to solve, and what new ethical or environmental problems might they create? Ultimately, is the future of the road paved at all, or does something more radical await over the horizon?

solar roadways | piezoelectric materials | inductive charging
DSRC | self-healing asphalt | modular pavement | permeable pavement

links.label: TRB induction healing of porous asphalt

entries.studentExplanation:
Future roads may not simply carry vehicles; they may generate energy, charge vehicles, communicate safety data, repair early cracks, or absorb stormwater. But a smarter road is not automatically a better road. Each innovation asks a trade-off question: does it actually solve a real problem at scale, or does it create new costs, surveillance concerns, repair burdens, and environmental risks?

Smart-road ideas should be judged by maintenance and scale, not just novelty. Piezoelectric roads, inductive charging, DSRC, self-healing asphalt, modular pavement, solar surfaces, and permeable pavement each solve one problem while creating cost, durability, surveillance, or repair questions. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand roads as active technologies rather than passive surfaces. At the section level, it moves the guiding section from road history to future infrastructure. At the year-theme level, it connects to progress because it asks whether the road to the future should be more technological, more resilient, or simply better maintained.
entries.takeaway: Whether active infrastructure improves mobility enough to justify its trade-offs
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether futuristic roads truly improve mobility or simply shift cost, repair, surveillance, and environmental burdens elsewhere.
entries.counterargument: Some smart-road ideas may stay niche because they are too expensive, fragile, complex, or difficult to maintain outside special settings.

entries.sectionTitle: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them

entries.title: Shortcuts, Scenic Routes, and the Logic of the Best Path
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText:
Urban designers know the frustration of watching unofficial trails sprout on perfectly manicured lawns. These are desire paths: the physical evidence of the shortest distance between two points. But, while a straight line is efficient, it isn’t always optimal. The “best” route is a shifting balance of speed, energy, and the unknown. With your team, consider the logic of the shortcut and the science of the scenic route. How do humans, animals, and even slime molds decide what way to go? Should we always follow the path of least resistance?

Traveling salesman problem | vector-based navigation | explore-exploit problem
Route elasticity | Braess’s Paradox | dead reckoning | the Steiner Tree Problem
entries.studentExplanation:
A desire path appears when people repeatedly choose the route that feels most natural, even if planners expected them to walk somewhere else. This makes shortcuts a kind of evidence: they show what people actually do, not just what designs told them to do. But the shortest route is not always the best one. Real route choices can balance speed, safety, energy, uncertainty, learning, crowding, and purpose.

This entry should compare optimization with human preference. The shortest path, scenic route, desire path, traveling-salesman logic, explore-exploit trade-off, Braess’s paradox, and dead reckoning all show that the “best” route depends on what a traveler values. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students question the idea that efficiency always means straightness. At the section level, it shifts from roads as objects to routes as choices. At the year-theme level, it connects to journeys because getting there depends not only on the destination, but also on how people decide which path is worth taking.
entries.takeaway: The best path is often a balance of speed, effort, knowledge, risk, and purpose
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether the best route is always the shortest route, or whether route choice should include risk, discovery, memory, and energy.
entries.counterargument: Efficiency still matters in many journeys, so detours and scenic routes are not automatically wiser than direct routes.

entries.title: Why Stroads Fail
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText: If the chicken crossed the stroad, it would probably get run over. Explore what makes stroads different from—and, according to critics, more dangerous than—traditional streets and roads, then discuss with your team: are there any in your community, and are there times when they might be the best choice?
entries.studentExplanation:
A stroad is a hybrid that tries to be a fast road and a local street at the same time. That sounds efficient, but critics argue it often fails at both: cars move too fast for a place full of driveways, shops, and pedestrians, while the space feels too dangerous and unpleasant to work as a real street. A stroad is a design problem because it mixes purposes that pull against each other.

A stroad fails because it tries to be a fast road and a productive street at the same time. The result is often dangerous speed, many driveways, weak walking conditions, expensive infrastructure, and places that are neither pleasant destinations nor efficient movement corridors. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand why some ordinary-looking roads feel stressful or unsafe. At the section level, it adds a real urban-design failure to the discussion of routes and infrastructure. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds and navigation because badly designed movement spaces can make everyday journeys harder instead of easier.
entries.takeaway: It shows how compromise can fail when goals are not truly compatible
examples: How a stroad can be repaired
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether car-oriented roads can be repaired into places that serve drivers, pedestrians, businesses, and communities at the same time.
entries.counterargument: Some stroads exist because communities need both movement and access, even if the design often handles both poorly.

entries.sectionTitle: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them

entries.title: The Road as a State of Mind
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText:
“It’s a dangerous business, Frodo, going out your door,” says Bilbo to his nephew in The Fellowship of the Ring, and he doesn’t even have stroads to worry about. Stepping over your threshold means signing a contract with the unknown, even if you have a destination in mind. In the works below, the road is not just a strip of dirt or asphalt: it is a psychological landscape. As you read them, consider: what is the difference between traveling by choice and by necessity? And is it ever better to just stay home?

Du Fu | “Travelling Again” (761 CE)
William Shakespeare | “Sonnet 50” (1609)
John Masefield | “Sea Fever” (1902)
Carl Sandburg | “Window” (1914)
Robert Frost | “The Road Not Taken” (1915)
William Butler Yeats | “Sailing to Byzantium” (1927)
Rustu Onur | “The Journey” (c. early 20th century)
Czeslaw Milosz | “This Only” (1988)
Richard Russo | “Just Drive” (1992)
Rita Dove | “Vacation” (1994)
entries.studentExplanation:
In these works, the road is not just a surface under someone’s feet. It becomes longing, tiredness, freedom, exile, duty, temptation, memory, or fear. Some speakers choose movement; others are pushed by loss, duty, age, or restlessness. The road can promise adventure, but it can also reveal loneliness and the cost of leaving home.

The poems make the road psychological. Du Fu, Shakespeare, Masefield, Frost, and other road texts turn movement into longing, fatigue, choice, exile, duty, temptation, or memory, so students should ask what state of mind each road creates. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students read roads as emotional symbols. At the section level, it shifts the guiding section from engineering to inner life. At the year-theme level, it connects to home, wandering, and thresholds because leaving the doorstep always means entering uncertainty.
entries.takeaway: A road can be both a physical path and a psychological landscape
examples: Robert Frost - The Road Not Taken
W. B. Yeats - Sailing to Byzantium
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether road literature treats movement as escape, burden, transformation, or a test of the self.
entries.counterargument: Poetry can make travel feel profound, but it can also turn hardship, loneliness, and uncertainty into something easier to romanticize.

entries.title: Why the Road Trip Became American
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText: While the first long-distance car journey took place in Germany, it was in America that the concept of the road trip took hold. Read more about its history then discuss with your team: why is the idea of the road trip so associated with America even when other places also have expansive road systems? Is it because the country lacks trains and buses?
entries.studentExplanation:
Bertha Benz’s 1888 journey in Germany helped prove that the automobile could be useful beyond short demonstrations. But the road trip became especially powerful in the United States because roads, cars, distance, tourism, migration, and ideas of freedom all came together. The American road trip is not just a drive; it became a national story about space, independence, discovery, and self-direction.

Bertha Benz proves long car travel before the American myth, but the United States made the road trip culturally central through distance, highways, motels, cars, migration, music, and ideas of freedom. The road trip is infrastructure plus story. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students separate the first long-distance car journey from the later road-trip myth. At the section level, it shows how infrastructure becomes culture. At the year-theme level, it connects to journeys and destinations because some routes matter not only for where they lead, but for the identity they create.
entries.takeaway: It became more than transport: a story about movement, choice, and identity
examples: Bertha Benz and the first long car journey
Not just because of missing trains
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating why some travel myths become attached to particular countries, technologies, and landscapes.
entries.counterargument: The United States mythologized the road trip, but it did not invent long-distance automobile travel or meaningful overland journeys.

entries.title: Can a Bus Journey Be a Road Trip?
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText: Be sure to look into other long distance overland journeys, including the once-legendary London to Calcutta bus service and the still operational Transoceánica from Brazil to Peru. Can they be called road trips even if they don’t take place in cars? Or is it only a road trip if you can choose when and where to stop along the way?
entries.studentExplanation:
Long overland bus journeys show that road travel does not belong only to private cars. A bus from London to Calcutta or a route across South America can be epic, exhausting, and transformative. But they also raise a question: is a road trip about distance and landscape, or about the freedom to choose when to stop, where to turn, and how to shape the journey?

Long bus journeys test whether a road trip requires private control. London-to-Calcutta style routes or South American overland buses create distance, landscape, discomfort, and shared experience, but less freedom to stop or detour, so agency becomes the key variable. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students test the limits of a familiar term. At the section level, it complicates the road-trip myth by adding collective, scheduled, and non-car overland travel. At the year-theme level, it connects to journeys because freedom, not distance alone, may shape how travel feels.
entries.takeaway: A road trip may depend on agency and experience as much as on distance or vehicle
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether a road trip is defined by road, distance, vehicle, freedom, or the traveler's control over the journey.
entries.counterargument: A long bus journey can be transformative, but passengers usually control less of the route, stops, and timing than private-car travelers.

entries.title: Why Route 66 Still Matters
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText: The Federal Highway Administration of the United States calls it “the Mother Road”: others refer to it as the Main Street of America. Though no longer a major thoroughfare, Route 66 is still widely revered and remembered. Read about its history, or check out this video, then consider: what is the most famous road in your own country? Why do you think Route 66 still tugs at the imagination of travelers today?
entries.studentExplanation:
Route 66 is not famous today because it is the fastest way across the United States. It matters because it became a cultural route: a symbol of migration, car travel, diners, gas stations, motels, small towns, music, and an older dream of America on the move. It shows how a road can stop being essential infrastructure and still remain powerful in memory.

Route 66 matters after usefulness because it became a memory route. Diners, motels, gas stations, small towns, migration, music, postcards, and “Mother Road” mythology let a road remain culturally alive even after faster highways replace its practical job. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students see how roads become symbols. At the section level, it shifts from road function to road mythology. At the year-theme level, it connects to journeys and destinations because some places matter as stories even after they stop being the most practical route.
entries.takeaway: A road can survive as cultural myth long after its transport role declines
examples: A road after its main function
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether a road matters more as functioning infrastructure or as a cultural story people keep preserving.
entries.counterargument: Cultural memory can exaggerate Route 66's centrality compared with its current everyday transport role.

entries.title: Motels: Sleeping on the Road
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText: People on road trips needed a place to sleep at night. Early on, many simply pulled off the road and camped beside their cars; over time, a new kind of car-friendly hotel emerged to serve them. Read about the world’s first motel and the history of motels since, then consider: are there equivalents in your country—and have you ever stayed at one?
entries.studentExplanation:
When cars made long-distance road travel more common, travelers needed places to sleep without leaving the road world behind. The motel emerged as a motor hotel: a car-friendly form of lodging designed for easy access, parking, short stays, and continued movement. It is not simply a smaller hotel; it is architecture shaped by road culture.

The motel is architecture shaped by the car: parking near the room, easy highway access, short stays, and a promise that sleep is only a pause before more movement. It is not just a cheap hotel; it is lodging designed around road rhythm. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this shows how movement creates new building types. At the section level, it extends road culture from driving to stopping. At the year-theme level, it connects to home and wandering because motels are classic in-between spaces: places to pause without fully arriving.
entries.takeaway: New movement patterns create new architecture for people between home and destination
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating how transportation systems create new building types and new in-between social spaces.
entries.counterargument: Motels can seem ordinary now, but that ordinariness still reveals how movement reshaped hospitality and architecture.

entries.title: Songs, Paintings, and the Feeling of the Highway
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText:
Consider the art and music selections below: what aspects of being on the road do they reflect? Do they over-idealize the experience of traveling by car—or critique it unfairly?

Nat Cole | “Get Your Kicks on Route 66” (1946)
Edward Hopper | Western Motel (1957)
Edward Ruscha | Standard Station (1966)
Willie Nelson | “On the Road Again Song” (1980)
David Hockney | Pearblossom Highway (1985)
Tom Cochrane | “Life is a Highway” (1991)
Bic Runga | “Drive” (1996)
Norah Jones | “Come Away with Me” (2002)
entries.studentExplanation:
Road songs and road images can make travel feel exciting, lonely, glamorous, repetitive, free, fake, or intimate. Some works celebrate the highway, while others quietly reveal its stillness, sameness, or artificial charm. This entry asks whether road culture is being romanticized, criticized, or both at once.

The cultural works make highways emotional objects. Route 66 songs sell freedom and rhythm, Hopper makes roadside stillness lonely, Ruscha turns gas stations into clean icons, and road images can celebrate mobility while also showing sameness or isolation. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students compare how music and visual art translate travel into mood. At the section level, it adds emotional depth to the road-trip cluster. At the year-theme level, it connects to home and wandering because being on the road can feel like escape, performance, loneliness, or desire all at once.
entries.takeaway: Art can both celebrate and question the road as freedom, routine, loneliness, or fantasy
examples: Nat Cole - Get Your Kicks on Route 66
Willie Nelson - On the Road Again
Tom Cochrane - Life Is a Highway
Norah Jones - Come Away With Me
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether road culture in art and music is honest, exaggerated, nostalgic, critical, or all of these at once.
entries.counterargument: Some road songs and images romanticize car travel, but others reveal its loneliness, sameness, cost, and staged quality.

entries.title: Circles, Detours, and Why a Journey Matters
entries.guidingSection: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
entries.rawOfficialText: “Beware thoughts that come in the night,” writes William Least-Heat Moon, in the introduction to his 1982 road trip travelogue masterpiece, Blue Highways. Read the book’s first page here, then discuss with your team: do you agree with him that traveling in a circle has more purpose than traveling in a straight line? If you were given a car, unlimited gas (or endless EV charging), and all the time in the world, where would you want to drive it?
entries.studentExplanation:
Blue Highways suggests that a journey does not gain all its meaning from taking the shortest route. A circle, detour, or slower road can reveal more than a straight line from A to B. This entry turns the road into a philosophical question: is travel mainly about reaching a point, or about what happens to the traveler while moving through space and time?

Blue highways and circular journeys challenge the idea that the best route is straight. Detours can reveal people, places, and thoughts that speed hides, so this entry asks whether travel should be measured by arrival or by what the route changes in the traveler. Use this to treat roads as systems, not background: what the route is built to do, where it channels people or goods, how it changes choices, and why routes remain cultural even after their practical role changes.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students think about whether the value of a journey lies in arrival or experience. At the section level, it provides a reflective ending to the road-travel cluster. At the year-theme level, it connects to journeys, destinations, and liminality because it asks whether movement itself can carry meaning beyond the endpoint.
entries.takeaway: A journey can matter because it changes the traveler through time, motion, and attention
examples: Beware thoughts that come in the night
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether travel is justified by destination, route, attention, or personal transformation along the way.
entries.counterargument: Detours can be meaningful, but not every circular route is purposeful and sometimes the destination really does matter most.
